justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Authentication per consumer type correctly designed — JWT for mobile with local JWKS validation, API keys for partners with cache lookup. (2) Internal traffic separation fully addressed — dedicated internal ingress via private network/service mesh, explicitly bypassing the public gateway. (3) Business logic explicitly excluded — gateway boundaries state 'Contains ZERO business logic', pitfalls section reinforces 'Do NOT put business logic in the gateway'. (4) SPOF risk identified as critical severity with horizontal scaling and multi-AZ mitigation. (5) Webhook delivery for partners fully addressed — dedicated Webhook Dispatcher component with async event consumption, retry logic, exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, and HMAC signing. (6) flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present and well-articulated. (7) Technology recommendation includes build/buy consideration — recommends AWS API Gateway if on AWS, Kong/Envoy if on Kubernetes, explicitly advises against building custom from scratch.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough internal traffic separation — not just mentioned but fully designed with network-level isolation, mTLS, and explicit reasoning for why internal traffic must not route through the public gateway
Business logic exclusion is reinforced at multiple levels: component boundaries, pitfalls section, and rigidity points — making it very hard to miss
Webhook delivery is a first-class component with its own bounded context, async event-driven architecture, retry policy, dead-letter queue, and partner-facing delivery dashboard
SPOF risk correctly rated as critical severity with concrete mitigations (stateless horizontal scaling, multi-AZ, circuit breakers)
Phased migration strategy (shadow mode → per-route enforcement → removal of per-service auth) demonstrates operational maturity
Fail-open rate limiting decision is well-reasoned with explicit trade-off analysis and Phase 2 fallback plan
Gateway latency overhead explicitly quantified at <5ms with P99 monitoring recommendation — matches expected 1-5ms range
Rigidity points include security invariants (header injection trust model, external/internal boundary) that show deep understanding of gateway security
Could have more explicitly discussed vendor lock-in risk for managed gateway services (AWS API Gateway) — it is implied in the technology-agnostic flexibility point but not called out as a named risk
Domain model with bounded contexts and context map adds structural clarity beyond what the rubric requires

trials.agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "Design a unified API gateway architecture for a logistics platform serving three distinct consumer types (mobile, B2B partners, internal services). The gateway centralizes authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation — eliminating the current inconsistency where each service independently implements these cross-cutting concerns. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with three logical ingress paths, each enforcing consumer-specific policies before ro

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Authentication per consumer type correctly designed — JWT/OAuth2 for mobile, API key + HMAC for B2B partners, with composable auth pipeline selecting strategy by route tag. (2) Internal traffic separation fully addressed via split-gateway topology with separate external and internal gateways on isolated network planes, enforced at network level with Kubernetes NetworkPolicies. (3) Business logic explicitly excluded — listed as first pitfall to avoid: 'Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it is an infrastructure concern only.' (4) SPOF risk identified as critical severity with detailed mitigation (horizontal scaling, health checks, blue-green deployments, load testing at 3x peak). (5) Webhook delivery for partners comprehensively addressed with dedicated Webhook Dispatcher component, async event consumption, exponential backoff retry, HMAC signature verification, dead-letter queue, and per-partner circuit breaker. (6) flexibility_points (auth strategies, rate limit tiers, webhook event types, gateway technology) and rigidity_points (traffic isolation, auth pipeline ordering, trusted header contract) both present and well-reasoned. (7) Technology recommendation covers Kong, Envoy/Istio, AWS API Gateway, and custom options with clear guidance that choice depends on team expertise and infrastructure platform — build vs buy consideration addressed.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough split-gateway design with network-level isolation, not just application-level ACLs — correctly identifies ACL misconfiguration risk as justification
Header spoofing risk identified as critical — a subtle but important gateway security concern that many designs miss
Strangler fig migration strategy is pragmatic and well-reasoned for transitioning from per-service auth to gateway auth
Redis failure mode analysis is nuanced — addresses the fail-open vs fail-closed dilemma with a time-boxed policy
Webhook delivery design is production-grade with exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, per-partner circuit breaker, and explicit isolation from API availability
Rigidity points are genuinely structural invariants rather than arbitrary constraints — auth pipeline ordering and header stripping are correctly identified as non-negotiable
Implementation guidance provides a realistic phased rollout plan with specific week-by-week milestones
Latency overhead per hop is mentioned only indirectly (~1-2ms for rate limiting) rather than explicitly calling out total gateway latency addition as a trade-off

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Authentication per consumer type correctly designed — JWT/OAuth2 for mobile, API key + HMAC for B2B partners, with strategy pattern for extensibility. (2) Internal traffic separation fully addressed — dedicated Internal Gateway on separate network interface/subnet, explicitly unreachable from public internet. (3) Business logic explicitly excluded — auth pipeline boundaries state 'No business logic', gateway handles only cross-cutting concerns. (4) SPOF risk identified as critical with horizontal scaling mitigation. (5) Webhook delivery for partners addressed via dedicated Webhook Dispatcher with event bus, exponential backoff retry, dead-letter queue, and per-partner circuit breaker. (6) flexibility_points (4 entries) and rigidity_points (3 entries) both present and well-reasoned. observations.text: Exceptionally thorough internal vs external traffic separation — not just mentioned but fully designed with network-level isolation, separate listeners, and defense-in-depth rationale

Webhook delivery design goes well beyond minimum — includes async event bus, exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, per-partner circuit breaker, and HMAC signature verification for partner webhook payloads
Explicit header sanitization (strip X-Consumer-* from incoming requests) addresses a subtle but critical security concern that many designs miss
Phased migration strategy with shadow mode is practical and reduces rollout risk — shows operational maturity beyond just architecture
Redis failure mode explicitly addressed with fail-open + alerting strategy and local fallback rate limiter — avoids the common trap of ignoring infrastructure dependency failures
Trade-offs section is genuinely useful — each decision includes concrete alternatives considered with clear reasoning for why they were rejected
Identity envelope contract identified as a rigidity point (shared kernel between gateway and backends) — correctly flags the coordination cost of changing internal headers
Latency-per-hop trade-off not explicitly quantified — the rubric expected 'typically 1-5ms' or similar concrete numbers for gateway overhead
Vendor lock-in risk for managed gateway services (e.g., AWS API Gateway) not called out as a named risk, though the technology guidance implicitly acknowledges the choice exists

  "executive_summary": "Design a unified API gateway architecture for a logistics platform serving three consumer tiers (mobile, B2B partner, internal). The architecture uses a single gateway with distinct listener/route groups per consumer type, an anti-corruption layer to shield internal services, and a plugin-based pipeline for cross-cutting concerns
